business_ideas.idea: Dog supplement brand - pivot from human vitamins to pet nutrition
Unclaimed baggage arbitrage - sell airport baggage via thrift retail
Multi-state residency optimization for tax purposes

predictions.text: More business ideas and profitable models exist in unclaimed/abandoned goods than most entrepreneurs realize

dynamics: Sam drives discovery through curiosity and research rabbit holes; Shaan engages intellectually and contributes parallel examples. Both exploring how unsexy businesses work—luggage, unclaimed goods, tax residency rules. Energetic and exploratory; Sam methodical, Shaan reflective.
frameworks: Frame-breaking principle—most profitable businesses deliberately ignore what looks impressive (dog vitamins > human beauty products)
Curiosity rabbit hole—understanding how unsexy systems work reveals massive opportunity
Capitalism as education—learning how commerce works teaches how everything else works
learnings: Customers for pet supplements cannot judge efficacy (unlike humans), creating margin without proof requirements
Abandoned airport baggage creates $300M/year business with zero brand awareness or marketing costs
Tax residency determined by 'teddy bear test'—where would you keep meaningful possessions, not days-count formulas
IRS operates at federal level; state taxes use possession/school/work/family proximity as proof
Understanding unglamorous business systems (luggage loading, baggage claims) reveals economic truth about commerce

banger_quotes.quote: Dogs will never tell you if vitamins work—that's the business model
If you don't care about taxes yet, you ain't rich
I'm Googling this for a friend and to enforce the law on my friend

numbers_dropped: Dog supplement brand: pivoted from skincare ($10M revenue) to pet focus
Unclaimed Baggage (rural Alabama): $300M annual revenue, operates as thrift store
New York apartment rental: ~$20k/month differential in taxes vs Texas/Florida residency
notable: Episode emphasizes how boring, unglamorous business models (vitamins for dogs that don't need verification, abandoned luggage retail) generate more profit than flashy ventures—classic MFM pattern of finding value in what others ignore
